_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic criteria per Fried et al. 2001 (PMID 11253156), Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (Rockwood 2005 PMID 16129869), and Rockwood Frailty Index (Mitnitski 2001 PMID 11581376). Prevalence per Collard 2012 (PMID 22881367) and Hoogendijk 2019 Lancet (PMID 31609228). Exercise evidence per Cadore 2014 (PMID 24788761). FRAIL scale per Morley 2012.

h1Title: Frailty Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Frailty syndrome is a clinical state of increased vulnerability to stressors in older adults caused by decline across multiple physiological systems. Pooled prevalence in community-dwelling adults aged 65 and over is roughly 11% for frailty and 42% for pre-frailty, rising to 38% in those over 90 and 75% in nursing home residents. The most widely used clinical definition (Fried physical phenotype) requires three or more of unintentional weight loss, exhaustion, weakness on grip-strength testing, slow walking speed, and low physical activity. Frail older adults face 1.5-3 times higher risk of falls, disability, hospitalisation, postoperative complications, and death compared with robust peers. Identification through brief tools (Clinical Frailty Scale, FRAIL questionnaire, gait speed) is now embedded in surgical, oncological, and primary care pathways, and structured progressive resistance and balance exercise combined with comprehensive geriatric assessment can reverse pre-frailty in 30-50% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 11% community frailty in adults aged 65+; 42% pre-frailty; rises to 38% over age 90 and 75% in nursing home residents (Collard 2012)
keyStats.demographics: Female:male ratio approximately 1.5:1; higher prevalence with lower income and education
keyStats.avgAge: Frailty rises exponentially after age 70; mean age of incident frailty 75-85 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 50-100 million older adults globally meet criteria for frailty; tens of millions more for pre-frailty

definition: Frailty syndrome (ICD-10: R54) is a clinical state of increased vulnerability in older adults that arises from age-related decline in reserve and function across multiple physiological systems (musculoskeletal, immune, neuroendocrine, cardiovascular, metabolic). It is distinct from disability (the inability to perform activities of daily living) and comorbidity (the presence of multiple chronic diseases) but overlaps with both. Two complementary frameworks dominate: the Fried physical phenotype, which defines frailty as three or more of unintentional weight loss above 4.5 kg in a year, self-reported exhaustion, weakness on hand grip-strength testing, slow gait speed below 0.8 m/s, and low self-reported physical activity (one or two of these defines pre-frailty); and the Rockwood deficit-accumulation model, which counts deficits (symptoms, signs, diseases, disabilities) and expresses frailty as a continuous index from 0 to 1. The Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS) is a 9-point visual judgement scale developed by Rockwood and validated in multiple settings — 1-3 represents fit to managing well, 4 living with very mild frailty, 5-7 mild to severe frailty, 8 very severe, 9 terminally ill. Frailty is now recognised as a geriatric syndrome that predicts adverse outcomes independent of chronological age and chronic disease burden. Management is led by geriatric medicine in partnership with primary care, surgery, oncology, and rehabilitation services.

typesClassification.description: One or two Fried criteria present, or Clinical Frailty Scale 3. Reversible state; structured exercise and nutritional support prevent transition to frailty in 30-50% of cases.
Three or more of weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slow walking, low activity. Predicts falls, disability, hospitalisation, and death.
Slowing down with need for help in high-order instrumental activities of daily living. Largely independent for basic self-care.
Help needed for outside activities and personal care (CFS 6) progressing to complete dependence for personal care without being at the end of life (CFS 7).
Approaching the end of life — CFS 8 completely dependent and not recovering even from minor illness; CFS 9 terminally ill with life expectancy under 6 months.

primarySymptoms: Unintentional weight loss greater than 4.5 kg (10 lbs) or 5% of body weight in the past year.
Self-reported exhaustion: feeling everything is an effort or that you cannot get going for at least 3 days a week.
Reduced hand grip strength below age- and sex-adjusted thresholds (lowest 20% on Jamar dynamometry).
Slow walking speed under 0.8 m/s over a 4-metre course at usual pace.
Low physical activity, with weekly energy expenditure below the lowest 20% by sex.
Increasing difficulty with instrumental activities of daily living such as shopping, cooking, handling finances, and using transport.
Multiple falls or near-falls in the past year, often without obvious cause.
Slowed cognition, low mood, social withdrawal, and reduced engagement in usual activities.
Frequent hospital admissions or delayed recovery from minor illness or surgery.
earlyWarningSigns: Loss of 2-3 kg over a year without dieting
Slower walking pace noticed by family or measured below 1 m/s
Need to use the arms to rise from a chair
Reduced grip when opening jars or holding a cup
Less time spent walking outside, gardening, or doing housework
emergencySigns: New confusion, drowsiness, or delirium developing over hours to days — exclude infection, dehydration, drug-induced delirium
Acute immobility with inability to weight-bear after a fall — exclude hip or vertebral fracture
Severe weight loss with new loss of appetite, vomiting, or pain — exclude undiagnosed malignancy or systemic infection
Acute severe dehydration with low blood pressure, tachycardia, and oliguria
Sudden disability after stroke, head injury, or pneumonia in a previously frail patient

causes.cause: Sarcopenia — age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength
causes.description: Skeletal muscle mass declines by 1-2% per year after age 50, with strength declining 2-4% per year. Sarcopenia is the central biological driver of physical frailty, linking inactivity, poor nutrition, and hormonal change to reduced grip strength, slow gait, and falls.

Persistently elevated IL-6, CRP, and TNF-α drive muscle catabolism, anorexia, and depression in older adults. Higher inflammatory marker levels predict incident frailty over 5 years independently of chronic disease.

Decline in growth hormone, IGF-1, sex steroids (testosterone in men, estradiol in women), and DHEA-S contributes to muscle and bone loss. Cortisol patterns flatten with ageing, worsening anabolic-catabolic balance.

More than 3 chronic conditions and 5 or more daily medications double the risk of frailty. Specific drugs (opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, sedating antihistamines) accelerate functional decline through falls and cognitive impairment.

causes.cause: Poor nutrition, low protein intake, and micronutrient deficiency
causes.description: Energy intake under 21 kcal/kg/day, protein intake under 0.8 g/kg/day, and deficiency of vitamin D, vitamin B12, folate, magnesium, and zinc each independently predict incident frailty.

Sedentary time above 8 hours/day is a strong, independent risk factor for frailty even in older adults who meet weekly aerobic guidelines. Loss of resistance and balance training accelerates sarcopenia and falls.

Cognitive frailty (frailty plus mild cognitive impairment) carries doubled risk of disability and mortality. Late-life depression reduces appetite, activity, and adherence, perpetuating the frailty cycle.
Living alone, social isolation, low income, and lower education each independently predict frailty incidence. Loneliness raises mortality at a level comparable to smoking 15 cigarettes daily.

riskFactors.description: Frailty prevalence rises from 4% at age 65-69 to 38% over age 90 (Collard 2012).
Women have 1.3-1.7 times higher prevalence of frailty than men of the same age, partly because of longer life expectancy and earlier postmenopausal muscle loss.
Diabetes, COPD, heart failure, kidney disease, and arthritis each independently double the risk of incident frailty. Disease control reduces but does not eliminate this risk.
Each additional daily medication beyond 5 raises frailty risk by 5-10%, especially opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, antipsychotics, and sedating antihistamines.

riskFactors.factor: Low physical activity and sedentary lifestyle
riskFactors.description: Failure to meet WHO physical activity guidelines (150 min moderate activity weekly plus resistance training twice weekly) triples the incidence of frailty.
Low protein intake, vitamin D deficiency, and low fruit and vegetable consumption predict incident frailty within 5 years.
Mild cognitive impairment doubles the risk of incident frailty; depression has a 2-3 fold association.

riskFactors.factor: Social isolation and low socioeconomic status
riskFactors.description: Living alone, limited social network, and low income each raise the incidence of frailty by 30-60% in longitudinal cohorts.
Hospitalisation for any reason raises frailty risk by 50% in the following year through bed rest deconditioning, infection, and medication changes.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 65 and over have 11% community prevalence of frailty and 42% pre-frailty (Collard 2012 meta-analysis).
Frailty prevalence rises to 38% over age 90 and 75% in nursing-home residents.
Women have higher frailty prevalence than men of the same age (15% vs 11%).
Lower-income and lower-education older adults have 2-fold higher prevalence than highest socioeconomic groups.
Surgical populations: 25-50% of older patients undergoing major surgery meet frailty criteria, predicting postoperative complications and mortality.
diagnosisOverview: Assessment uses a combination of brief screening and comprehensive geriatric assessment. Screening tools take 1-5 minutes: the FRAIL questionnaire (5 items — Fatigue, Resistance to climbing stairs, Ambulation 1 block, Illnesses ≥5, Loss of weight ≥5%); the Clinical Frailty Scale (9-point visual judgement scale based on patient history and examination); and gait speed over 4 metres (≤0.8 m/s indicates frailty). Confirmed frailty triggers a comprehensive geriatric assessment by a geriatrician or geriatric specialist nurse covering physical health (chronic diseases, vision, hearing, dentition, continence), mental health (cognition with MMSE/MoCA, mood with PHQ-9 or GDS), functional status (Barthel index for ADL, Lawton scale for IADL), social circumstances (informal support, housing, finances), and a medication review with deprescribing where appropriate. Investigations target reversible contributors: full blood count, ferritin, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, thyroid function, renal function, glucose, HbA1c, calcium, and albumin; targeted imaging or specialist referral for new weight loss, anaemia, or falls. The Rockwood Frailty Index counts deficits from a 30- to 70-item list and produces a continuous score (frailty index ≥0.25 indicates frailty); it is increasingly used in research and electronic health records. Surgical and oncological pathways now use frailty assessment to risk-stratify and modify treatment intensity, including the use of the Edmonton Frail Scale, ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator, and the G8 geriatric oncology screening tool.

diagnosticTests.test: FRAIL questionnaire and Clinical Frailty Scale
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid clinical screening for frailty in primary care, emergency, and pre-operative settings
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5 yes/no questions for FRAIL; 1-9 visual judgement for CFS. Total time 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Simple, validated marker of frailty (≤0.8 m/s) and mortality risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient walks 4 metres at usual pace; the clinician times with a stopwatch. Takes 1-2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Component of the Fried phenotype; tracks sarcopenia response to interventions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three measurements per hand at full elbow extension; mean of best result used. Takes 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and addresses reversible medical, functional, mental, and social contributors to frailty
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 60-90 minute structured assessment by geriatrician or specialist team; may be split across visits.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests: full blood count, B12, folate, vitamin D, ferritin, thyroid, renal, calcium, albumin, HbA1c
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects reversible contributors — anaemia, vitamin deficiency, thyroid disease, malnutrition, undiagnosed diabetes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Cognitive and mood screening (MMSE/MoCA, PHQ-9 or GDS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cognitive impairment and depression which drive cognitive frailty
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief structured interview or self-completed questionnaire. 10-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Functional assessment (Barthel index, Lawton IADL scale)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies dependence in activities of daily living and informs care planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient or carer questionnaire completed during clinic visit. 10-15 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Management has four pillars: structured exercise, optimised nutrition, comprehensive geriatric assessment with deprescribing, and treatment of reversible contributors. Multicomponent exercise — progressive resistance training (2-3 sessions weekly), aerobic activity (150 minutes weekly of moderate intensity), balance work, and flexibility — is the single intervention with the strongest evidence: a Cochrane meta-analysis of randomised trials showed reductions in frailty status (number needed to treat 6-12) and improved gait speed, grip strength, and falls risk. Protein intake should rise to 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day during illness or post-surgery) with adequate energy and distributed across meals; oral nutritional supplements with leucine-enriched whey protein improve muscle mass and function in malnourished patients. Vitamin D supplementation 800-1,000 IU/day raises serum 25-OH-vitamin-D above 50 nmol/L and reduces falls in deficient patients. Comprehensive geriatric assessment by a multidisciplinary team reduces nursing-home admission, functional decline, and mortality in hospital inpatients (NNT roughly 13). Medication review using STOPP/START or Beers criteria identifies high-risk medications (long-acting benzodiazepines, sedating antihistamines, anticholinergics) for deprescribing, with structured tapering to reduce withdrawal symptoms. Treat reversible contributors: iron, B12 or folate-deficiency anaemia; hypothyroidism; uncontrolled diabetes; chronic pain; depression with SSRIs or talking therapy. Social prescribing, fall-prevention programmes, advance care planning, and proactive influenza, pneumococcal, and shingles vaccination round out the package. Surgical and oncological care should consider prehabilitation (4-6 weeks of structured exercise and nutrition before elective surgery) in frail patients to reduce complications by 30-50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Multicomponent exercise programme (resistance + aerobic + balance + flexibility)
medicalTreatments.description: 2-3 supervised sessions per week of progressive resistance training plus 150 minutes weekly moderate aerobic activity and balance training (tai chi, Otago programme). Continued at home with periodic supervision.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces frailty status, improves gait speed by 0.10-0.15 m/s, halves falls in pre-frail older adults (Cadore 2014; Cochrane 2020)

medicalTreatments.name: Optimised protein and energy intake plus oral nutritional supplements
medicalTreatments.description: Aim for 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day of protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day during illness), with leucine-enriched whey protein supplements where intake is inadequate. Adequate total energy (25-30 kcal/kg/day).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases muscle mass by 0.5-1 kg over 12 weeks; reduces hospitalisation when combined with exercise (PROVIDE trial)

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation 800-1,000 IU/day
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended in older adults with low or unmeasured 25-OH-vitamin-D. Single doses above 100,000 IU should be avoided.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 20-30% reduction in falls in vitamin-D-deficient adults (Cochrane 2018); modest functional benefit when combined with exercise

medicalTreatments.name: Comprehensive geriatric assessment by multidisciplinary team
medicalTreatments.description: Structured holistic assessment of medical, functional, mental, and social health by geriatrician, nurse, physiotherapist, occupational therapist, pharmacist, and social worker. Repeated at major care transitions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hospital functional decline by 20%, nursing-home admission by 18%, and mortality by 9% (Ellis 2017 Cochrane; NNT 13)

medicalTreatments.name: Medication review with STOPP/START criteria and structured deprescribing
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces high-risk medications (long-acting benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, sedating antihistamines, opioids), simplifies regimens, and aligns with patient priorities. Pharmacist-led reviews especially effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 20-30% reduction in falls and hospitalisations in randomised trials; preferred medication discontinuation rate 30-60%

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of reversible contributors (anaemia, vitamin deficiency, thyroid disease, depression, pain)
medicalTreatments.description: Address iron, B12, folate, vitamin D deficiency; treat hypothyroidism, uncontrolled diabetes, chronic pain, and depression with first-line therapies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Measurable functional improvement when single contributor identified and treated; variable benefit overall

surgicalOptions.name: Prehabilitation before elective surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Structured 4-6 week programme of progressive resistance and aerobic exercise plus nutritional optimisation in frail patients awaiting cardiac, abdominal, or orthopaedic surgery. Co-delivered by physiotherapy and dietetics.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-50% reduction in postoperative complications, shorter hospital stay, and faster return to baseline function in randomised trials (Carli 2018)

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi and other mind-body exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Tai chi 2-3 sessions per week reduces falls by 20-30% in older adults and improves balance, fear of falling, and quality of life. Especially useful in frail patients with anxiety or fall-related avoidance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Social prescribing and structured peer support
alternativeTreatments.description: Linking older adults to community activities, befriending services, and group exercise classes reduces loneliness and supports adherence to exercise and nutrition interventions. Increasingly embedded in primary care.

whySeeSpecialist: Frailty assessment is part of routine primary care for older adults. Geriatric specialist referral is warranted when frailty is moderate to severe, when multiple reversible contributors are suspected, before major elective surgery, after multiple unplanned admissions, or when complex deprescribing is needed. Surgical and oncological specialists should integrate frailty assessment into treatment planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a geriatrician or specialist clinical service with experience in comprehensive geriatric assessment, multidisciplinary working, and proactive care planning. Ask whether the service offers prehabilitation, structured deprescribing, and fall prevention programmes. Look for clinicians familiar with the Clinical Frailty Scale and Rockwood Frailty Index.
hospitalCriteria: Geriatric medicine service with comprehensive geriatric assessment capability
Multidisciplinary team including geriatrician, specialist nurse, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, pharmacist, and social worker
Falls clinic and bone health service
Prehabilitation programme integrated with surgical and oncological pathways
keyFacilities: Hospice and palliative care services for end-of-life frailty
preventionStrategies: Engage in resistance, aerobic, balance, and flexibility exercise at least three times weekly from middle age onwards.
Maintain protein intake of 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day with adequate total energy and good dietary quality.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to under 14 units per week.
Treat chronic diseases proactively — diabetes, hypertension, COPD, heart failure, kidney disease — to reduce frailty risk.
Use vision and hearing aids and attend regular check-ups; sensory impairment accelerates frailty.
Stay socially engaged — clubs, faith groups, volunteering — to reduce isolation and loneliness.
lifestyleModifications: Perform progressive resistance exercise (using weights or resistance bands) two to three times weekly.
Aim for 30 minutes of moderate aerobic activity (brisk walking, gardening) on most days of the week.
Include balance work (tai chi, single-leg stance) two to three times weekly.
Eat protein-rich foods at every meal (eggs, dairy, fish, lean meat, legumes).
Take vitamin D 800-1,000 IU daily if levels are low or sun exposure limited.
Review all medicines annually with a pharmacist or GP to deprescribe unnecessary or high-risk drugs.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet with vegetables, fruits, fish, olive oil, nuts, and legumes
Protein-rich foods at every meal (eggs, dairy, lean meat, fish, beans) for total 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day
Oral nutritional supplements when appetite is poor or weight loss is unintentional
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L/day unless fluid restriction is advised)
avoid: Highly processed foods low in micronutrients
Skipping meals or prolonged fasting which accelerate muscle loss in older adults
Excess alcohol which raises falls, malnutrition, and cognitive impairment risk
Restrictive diets without medical supervision in older adults

exerciseGuidelines: Multicomponent exercise is the cornerstone of frailty management: progressive resistance training 2-3 times weekly (8-10 major muscle groups, 8-12 repetitions per set, 2-3 sets), 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity, balance training 2-3 times weekly (tai chi, Otago programme), and daily flexibility work. Supervised programmes are preferable for moderate-severe frailty; home-based programmes work for pre-frail and mildly frail individuals.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed resistance, balance, and aerobic exercises every day or every other day.
Eat protein-rich foods at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.
Take vitamin D and other prescribed supplements.
Use mobility aids (stick, frame) prescribed by a physiotherapist to maintain confidence and prevent falls.
Maintain regular daily routines including sleep, social contact, and outdoor time.
Attend regular reviews with the GP and geriatric service for medication and care plan updates.
prognosis: Frailty is a strong predictor of falls, disability, hospitalisation, postoperative complications, and death. Compared with robust older adults, frail individuals have 1.5-3 fold higher 5-year mortality after adjusting for age and comorbidity. However, frailty is not a one-way trajectory. In longitudinal studies, 23-40% of pre-frail individuals transition back to robust status over 3-5 years when active management is provided. Multicomponent exercise plus nutrition can reverse pre-frailty in 30-50% of older adults and reduce functional decline in those already frail. Comprehensive geriatric assessment improves outcomes after acute hospitalisation, reducing nursing-home admissions by 18% and mortality by 9%. Prehabilitation before major surgery cuts complications by 30-50%. Even in very severe frailty (CFS 8-9), proactive palliative care, advance care planning, and avoidance of harmful medications improve quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: Strength and gait speed begin to improve within 6-8 weeks of starting structured exercise and nutrition. Maximum functional gains usually appear by 12-26 weeks. Transition from pre-frail to robust takes 3-6 months in most responders. After hospitalisation, regaining baseline function may take 6-12 months. Long-term maintenance requires continued exercise, nutrition, and medication review.
complications: Falls, hip fracture, and other fragility fractures — assess falls history, vision, footwear, home hazards, and bone health.
Recurrent unplanned hospital admissions with deconditioning and hospital-associated functional decline.
Delirium during acute illness or hospitalisation, especially superimposed on cognitive impairment.
Pressure ulcers and venous thromboembolism during periods of immobility.
Adverse drug events from polypharmacy, particularly anticholinergic, sedative, and analgesic burden.
Disability, loss of independence, and admission to long-term residential care.
Increased postoperative complications and 30-day mortality after major surgery.

supportResources.name: British Geriatrics Society — Fit for Frailty
supportResources.description: UK clinical guidance and patient-facing resources on frailty recognition and management.

supportResources.name: American Geriatrics Society — HealthInAging
supportResources.description: Patient-facing site from the AGS with information on frailty, falls, and healthy ageing.

supportResources.name: International Conference on Frailty and Sarcopenia Research
supportResources.description: International academic conference and clinical resource hub.

supportResources.name: World Health Organization Decade of Healthy Ageing
supportResources.description: WHO framework and resources supporting healthy ageing and frailty prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcopenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcopenia is the age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength; frailty is a broader clinical syndrome including weight loss, exhaustion, and slow gait alongside sarcopenia. Sarcopenia is a major biological driver of frailty but does not include the multi-system features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Falls in Elderly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Falls are events; frailty is the underlying vulnerability that makes falls more likely. Many frail older adults present with recurrent falls, but falls also occur from acute illness, medications, or environmental hazards without frailty being present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Late-life depression and frailty share fatigue, weight loss, reduced activity, and social withdrawal. PHQ-9 or Geriatric Depression Scale screens identify treatable depression, which can present as frailty or worsen it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cognitive frailty (frailty plus mild cognitive impairment) overlaps with early dementia but is distinct: dementia has progressive memory and cognitive decline as the dominant feature, while frailty centres on physical decline. The two often coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Falls in Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Frailty is the single most important predictor of recurrent falls and fall-related injury in older adults.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcopenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Central biological driver of physical frailty; managed with the same exercise and nutrition strategy.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Often coexists with frailty and amplifies fracture risk; integrated bone health assessment is essential.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Common comorbidity producing cognitive frailty; assessment of cognition is part of geriatric assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Late-life depression mimics, worsens, and complicates frailty management.

faqs.question: What is frailty syndrome?
faqs.answer: Frailty syndrome is a clinical state of increased vulnerability in older adults caused by decline in reserve across multiple body systems. Hallmark features are unintentional weight loss, weakness, slow walking, exhaustion, and reduced physical activity. Frail older adults face higher risk of falls, hospitalisation, and death.

faqs.question: Is frailty the same as old age?
faqs.answer: No. Frailty describes a clinical state of vulnerability, not simply chronological age. Many older adults remain robust into their 80s and 90s, while some adults in their 60s become frail because of disease, inactivity, or poor nutrition. Frailty assessment captures this individual variation.

faqs.question: What are the signs of frailty?
faqs.answer: Common signs include unintended weight loss of 4.5 kg or more in a year, exhaustion, weak grip strength, slow walking speed (under 0.8 m/s), low physical activity, and difficulty with daily tasks like shopping, cooking, and bathing. Three or more of these features confirm frailty.

faqs.question: How is frailty diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Clinicians use brief screening tools (FRAIL questionnaire, Clinical Frailty Scale, 4-metre gait speed) followed by comprehensive geriatric assessment in confirmed cases. Blood tests rule out reversible contributors such as anaemia, vitamin deficiencies, and thyroid disease.

faqs.question: Can frailty be reversed?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially in pre-frailty and mild frailty. Around 30-50% of pre-frail older adults regain robust status with multicomponent exercise and improved nutrition over 3-6 months. Even severe frailty can be stabilised and complications reduced with comprehensive geriatric care.

faqs.question: What exercises help frailty?
faqs.answer: Multicomponent exercise programmes combining resistance training (2-3 sessions weekly), aerobic activity (150 minutes weekly), balance work (tai chi, Otago), and flexibility are the strongest evidence-based intervention. Programmes should be progressive and supervised when frailty is moderate or severe.

faqs.question: What should frail older adults eat?
faqs.answer: Aim for 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day of protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day during illness) distributed across meals, alongside a Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruits, olive oil, and oily fish. Vitamin D 800-1,000 IU/day is recommended in older adults with low or unmeasured levels.

faqs.question: What is the Clinical Frailty Scale?
faqs.answer: The Clinical Frailty Scale is a 9-point visual judgement scale developed by Kenneth Rockwood. It runs from 1 (very fit) through 3-4 (managing well, very mild frailty) to 7-8 (severe and very severe frailty) and 9 (terminally ill). It is widely used in hospitals, GP practices, and surgical preoperative pathways.

faqs.question: Does frailty affect surgery outcomes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Frail older adults face 2-3 times higher 30-day complications and mortality after major surgery compared with robust peers. Routine frailty screening before elective surgery and prehabilitation (4-6 weeks of structured exercise and nutrition) reduce complications by 30-50%.

faqs.question: Is frailty hereditary?
faqs.answer: Frailty itself is not directly inherited, but genetic factors influence muscle mass, bone density, and disease risk. Twin studies suggest 30-45% heritability of the frailty phenotype. Lifestyle and environmental factors carry more weight at the individual level.

faqs.question: How is frailty different from sarcopenia?
faqs.answer: Sarcopenia is the age-related loss of muscle mass and strength. Frailty is a broader clinical syndrome that includes sarcopenia plus weight loss, exhaustion, slow gait, and low activity across multiple body systems. Sarcopenia is the main biological driver of physical frailty.

faqs.question: Can medications worsen frailty?
faqs.answer: Yes. Long-acting benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, sedating antihistamines, opioids, and many antipsychotics increase falls, cognitive impairment, and functional decline. Annual medication review using STOPP/START criteria identifies high-risk drugs for safe deprescribing.

faqs.question: What is comprehensive geriatric assessment?
faqs.answer: Comprehensive geriatric assessment is a structured multidisciplinary evaluation of older adults covering medical, functional, mental, and social health. It identifies and treats reversible contributors to frailty, reduces hospital functional decline by 20%, and lowers nursing-home admissions by 18%.

faqs.question: Can frailty be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes, partly. Regular physical activity (especially resistance training), adequate protein intake, control of chronic diseases, treatment of vision and hearing impairment, social engagement, and avoidance of high-risk medications all reduce the incidence of frailty in older adults.

faqs.question: When should I see a geriatrician?
faqs.answer: Consider geriatric medicine review for moderate to severe frailty, frequent falls, multiple unplanned hospital admissions, complex medication regimens, before major elective surgery, or when cognitive concerns coexist with physical decline. Many GPs and primary care teams provide geriatric assessment.

faqs.question: Is frailty the end of independence?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Many frail older adults maintain independent living with proactive management. Multicomponent exercise, nutrition support, medication review, home modifications, and falls prevention reduce functional decline and delay or avoid the need for residential care.

faqs.question: Does frailty mean a person is dying?
faqs.answer: No. Most frail older adults live for years with appropriate care. Very severe frailty (Clinical Frailty Scale 8-9) is associated with limited life expectancy and often warrants palliative care planning. Even at this stage, comfort, dignity, and quality of life can be supported actively.

faqs.question: How does vitamin D help with frailty?
faqs.answer: Vitamin D supports muscle function and bone health. Supplementation 800-1,000 IU/day reduces falls by 20-30% in deficient older adults and supports the benefit of resistance training. Single very high doses above 100,000 IU should be avoided as they have been linked to higher fall risk.

faqs.question: What is cognitive frailty?
faqs.answer: Cognitive frailty is the combination of physical frailty and mild cognitive impairment without dementia. It carries a doubled risk of progression to dementia, disability, and death. Multicomponent exercise, cognitive stimulation, and management of depression slow progression.

faqs.question: Can frailty cause sudden falls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Frail older adults have 1.5-2 times higher risk of falls due to reduced muscle strength, slower gait, postural instability, and polypharmacy. Many falls occur during routine activities. Multifactorial fall prevention programmes (exercise, vision, footwear, home safety) reduce falls by 20-30%.

faqs.question: How can family help a frail older relative?
faqs.answer: Encourage and support regular exercise and balanced nutrition, accompany the relative to GP and geriatric appointments, help with medication management, address home hazards (loose rugs, poor lighting), keep them socially engaged, and seek advice on respite care and advance planning when needed.

metaTitle: Frailty Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Frailty syndrome: weight loss, weakness, slow walking in older adults. Diagnosis, exercise and nutrition treatment, prevention, and prognosis explained.
